category.label: Polynomial and Rational Functions
category.description: Analyzes polynomial and rational functions through covariation, rates of change, zeros, end behavior, asymptotes, transformations, and modeling. Covers CED Unit 1 (topics 1.1–1.14) of AP Precalculus, forming 30–40% of the exam and appearing in FRQs 1 and 2.
category.yield_rationale: Unit 1 is 30–40% of the AP Precalc exam — the largest and most variable share; FRQs 1 and 2 both draw heavily from it, and every topic from 1.1–1.14 is testable across both MC sections.

umbrellas.label: Change in Tandem (Covariation)
umbrellas.description: Describes how input and output values of a function co-vary across representations (graph, table, symbolic, context). Tests reading direction and relative magnitude of change, not rate calculation.
umbrellas.yield_rationale: CED 1.1 is foundational covariation reasoning; appears in FRQ 1 (function concepts) and numerous MC items requiring interpretation of increasing/decreasing behavior.

in_depth.label: Identifying co-variation direction from a graph
in_depth.description: Given a graph of a function, determines whether output increases, decreases, or remains constant as input increases over a specified interval.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Direct MC target for 1.1; reading increasing/decreasing intervals from a graph is a repeatable, isolatable skill.

in_depth.label: Identifying co-variation direction from a table
in_depth.description: Uses an input-output table to determine whether the function is increasing or decreasing and to compare output changes across rows.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Table-based covariation is tested in MC Part A (no calc); isolates table-reading skill separately from graph reading.

in_depth.label: Comparing co-variation across two representations
in_depth.description: Given the same function in two different representations (e.g., graph and table), identifies whether described co-variation features are consistent between them.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Multiple-representation comparison (MP2) is explicitly tested; medium frequency in MC items. Rescored downward (from 0.68): multi-rep comparison is scaffolding for other skills rather than a standalone isolated exam target.

in_depth.label: Interpreting co-variation in a real-world context
in_depth.description: Translates a verbal or contextual description of two co-varying quantities into a statement about function behavior (e.g., 'as time increases, height decreases').
in_depth.yield_rationale: Context-based covariation questions are common in MC and set up FRQ 2 modeling tasks.

umbrellas.label: Rates of Change
umbrellas.description: Calculates and interprets average rate of change of a function over an interval; connects sign of AROC to direction of co-variation and magnitude to steepness.
umbrellas.yield_rationale: CED 1.2 AROC calculation appears in FRQ 2 (non-periodic modeling) and multiple MC; one of the most frequently isolated computational skills in Unit 1.

in_depth.label: Interpreting the sign of AROC
in_depth.description: Determines whether a positive, negative, or zero average rate of change implies increasing, decreasing, or constant function behavior over an interval.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Sign interpretation is tested in MP3 communication items; bridges AROC calculation to function behavior.

in_depth.label: Interpreting AROC with units in context
in_depth.description: States the meaning of a computed average rate of change using correct units (e.g., 'feet per second') and relates it to the modeled situation.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Contextual interpretation is required in FRQ 2 explanatory sub-parts; tests MP3 communication practice. Rescored upward to separate from mid-range cluster.

in_depth.label: Comparing AROC over sub-intervals of a polynomial to infer concavity
in_depth.description: Computes or reads AROC values over multiple consecutive equal-width sub-intervals of a polynomial function and determines whether the AROC is increasing (concave up) or decreasing (concave down) — bridging CED 1.2 computation to the 1.3 concavity concept.
in_depth.yield_rationale: P1-exclusive: requires polynomial-context AROC comparison across sub-intervals, not just a single interval computation. Bridges CED 1.2 and 1.3; appears in MC items requiring justification of concavity direction.

in_depth.label: Using AROC sign changes to count or bound the number of local extrema
in_depth.description: Given a table of AROC values over successive intervals of a polynomial, identifies the sub-intervals where AROC changes sign (positive to negative or vice versa) and uses each sign change to locate a local extremum, thereby bounding the minimum number of turning points — a CED 1.4 skill applied within the AROC framework.
in_depth.yield_rationale: P1-exclusive: connects AROC sign-change reasoning (1.2) to extremum counting (1.4). Not a foundations-level skill; requires interpreting a pattern of AROC values across intervals of a polynomial rather than a single computation.

umbrellas.label: Rates of Change in Linear and Quadratic Functions
umbrellas.description: Distinguishes constant rate of change (linear) from non-constant rate of change (quadratic) by analyzing first and second differences in tables or descriptions; connects changing AROC to concavity.
umbrellas.yield_rationale: CED 1.3 provides the conceptual lens for distinguishing function families; referenced in FRQ 2 justification sub-parts about model selection.

in_depth.label: Identifying linearity from constant first differences
in_depth.description: Uses equally-spaced table values to verify that constant first differences imply a linear function; connects this to a constant rate of change.
in_depth.yield_rationale: First-difference test is a direct 1.3 skill; also prerequisite for recognizing polynomial degree from tables.

in_depth.label: Identifying quadratic behavior from constant second differences
in_depth.description: Checks that first differences change at a constant rate (constant second differences) to identify a quadratic relationship in a table or context.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Second-difference test isolates the quadratic vs linear distinction; appears in MC model-selection items.

in_depth.label: Connecting increasing/decreasing AROC to concave up/down
in_depth.description: Determines whether a function is concave up (AROC increasing) or concave down (AROC decreasing) from a graph or successive table differences.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Concavity-language appears in MC context questions and FRQ communication sub-parts; moderate frequency.

in_depth.label: Comparing AROC over different intervals for quadratic vs linear
in_depth.description: Given two functions (one linear, one quadratic) on the same interval, explains why the linear function has equal AROCs over equal sub-intervals while the quadratic does not.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Comparative reasoning between function families appears in MC justification items; slightly lower frequency than direct calculation items.

umbrellas.label: Polynomial Functions and Rates of Change
umbrellas.description: Describes rate-of-change behavior of polynomial functions over intervals; connects the number of sign changes of AROC to the degree, and identifies local extrema in terms of AROC changing sign.
umbrellas.yield_rationale: CED 1.4 bridges AROC to polynomial degree; moderate MC frequency and referenced in FRQ 2 when justifying degree of a polynomial model.

in_depth.label: Counting AROC sign changes to bound the number of turning points
in_depth.description: Uses the fact that a degree-n polynomial has at most n−1 local extrema (AROC sign changes) to infer a lower bound on degree from observed turning points.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Degree-from-behavior reasoning is a direct 1.4 MC target; connects degree to function shape.

in_depth.label: Determining the sign of AROC over an interval from a polynomial graph
in_depth.description: Reads a polynomial graph to state whether the AROC over a given interval is positive, negative, or zero by identifying whether the function rises or falls.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Graphical AROC-sign reading is a standard low-difficulty MC item for polynomial behavior.

in_depth.label: Inferring polynomial degree from a rate-of-change table
in_depth.description: Given a table of AROC values over successive equal-width intervals, determines the minimum polynomial degree that is consistent with the observed AROC pattern.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Rate-pattern-to-degree inference appears in MC items; requires integrating 1.3 finite-difference ideas with 1.4 degree concepts.

in_depth.label: Identifying local extrema as points where AROC changes sign
in_depth.description: Explains that a local maximum or minimum of a polynomial occurs where the average rate of change transitions from positive to negative (or vice versa), and locates such points on a graph.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Conceptual interpretation of extrema in AROC terms is tested in MP3 communication sub-parts.

umbrellas.label: Polynomial Functions and Complex Zeros
umbrellas.description: Identifies real and non-real (complex) zeros of polynomials using the Fundamental Theorem of Algebra; relates irreducible quadratic factors to pairs of complex conjugate zeros; determines the total zero count from degree.
umbrellas.yield_rationale: CED 1.5 complex zeros appear occasionally in MC but carry lower weight than end behavior or asymptote topics; less frequent in FRQs.

in_depth.label: Applying the Fundamental Theorem to count total zeros
in_depth.description: States that a degree-n polynomial has exactly n zeros (counting multiplicity and complex) and uses this to determine how many zeros must be non-real given the real zeros found.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Total-zero-count reasoning is a clean, isolatable MC skill for 1.5.

in_depth.label: Identifying complex zeros as conjugate pairs
in_depth.description: Recognizes that non-real zeros of a polynomial with real coefficients come in conjugate pairs (a + bi and a − bi), and uses this to write or factor the corresponding irreducible quadratic.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Conjugate-pair rule is a targeted MC item; less frequently tested than real-zero analysis.

in_depth.label: Recognizing an irreducible quadratic factor
in_depth.description: Determines whether a quadratic factor of a polynomial has a negative discriminant and therefore cannot be factored over the reals, contributing two complex zeros instead.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Discriminant-based irreducibility test is the computational gateway to identifying non-real zeros.

in_depth.label: Classifying zeros as real or non-real from a factored form
in_depth.description: Given a polynomial in factored or partially factored form, classifies each factor's zeros as real (x-intercepts) or non-real (no x-intercept), and states the total count of each type.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Classification questions at the intersection of zeros and graph behavior appear in MC items linking 1.5 to 1.6.

in_depth.label: Zero multiplicity controlling whether a rational function crosses or bounces at an x-intercept
in_depth.description: Determines whether the graph of a rational function crosses the x-axis (odd-multiplicity numerator factor at that zero) or bounces off it without crossing (even-multiplicity numerator factor), specifically for rational functions where the zero is not also a denominator root — a P1-exclusive skill distinct from the pure-polynomial case.
in_depth.yield_rationale: P1-exclusive: applies multiplicity reasoning to rational function zeros, where the interplay between numerator and denominator factors adds a layer of analysis not present for pure polynomials (already covered in foundations). Moderate MC frequency in rational-function graph-matching items.

umbrellas.label: Polynomial Functions and End Behavior
umbrellas.description: Determines the end behavior of a polynomial function from its degree and leading coefficient, describing how f(x) behaves as x → ±∞ using arrow notation and contextual language.
umbrellas.yield_rationale: CED 1.6 end behavior is heavily tested in MC and appears explicitly in FRQ 1 (function concepts task); among the highest-yield polynomial skills on the exam.

in_depth.label: Matching a polynomial graph to an end-behavior description
in_depth.description: Selects or draws the graph of a polynomial whose end behavior matches a given symbolic description (e.g., as x → −∞, f(x) → +∞; as x → +∞, f(x) → −∞).
in_depth.yield_rationale: Graph-matching items that isolate end behavior appear in MC Part A; tests the graphical representation skill.

in_depth.label: Identifying the leading term in an expanded polynomial for end behavior
in_depth.description: Given a polynomial in expanded (non-factored) form, identifies the leading term and infers end behavior without fully re-factoring the expression.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Extracting the leading term from a sum is a prerequisite procedural step tested in MC items before end-behavior conclusions.

umbrellas.label: Rational Functions and End Behavior
umbrellas.description: Determines horizontal asymptotes and end behavior of rational functions by comparing degrees of numerator and denominator polynomials; uses the leading-coefficient ratio or limit argument.
umbrellas.yield_rationale: CED 1.7 rational end behavior is a reliable MC pool topic and also tested in FRQ 1; degree-comparison rule is one of the most testable single-rule skills in Unit 1.

in_depth.label: Horizontal asymptote y = 0 when degree of numerator < degree of denominator
in_depth.description: States and applies the rule that if deg(numerator) < deg(denominator) the horizontal asymptote is y = 0, and describes end behavior accordingly.
in_depth.yield_rationale: The three HA cases are individually isolatable; this is the most distinctive case and frequently tested.

in_depth.label: Horizontal asymptote y = a/b when degrees are equal
in_depth.description: States and applies the rule that equal-degree numerator and denominator yield a horizontal asymptote at y = (leading coefficient of numerator) / (leading coefficient of denominator).
in_depth.yield_rationale: Equal-degree HA is the most common MC case for rational end behavior; the ratio computation is a clean isolated skill.

in_depth.label: No horizontal asymptote when degree of numerator > degree of denominator
in_depth.description: Identifies that when deg(numerator) > deg(denominator) there is no horizontal asymptote, and describes the resulting unbounded end behavior (polynomial-like behavior after division).
in_depth.yield_rationale: No-HA case appears in MC items testing all three degree-comparison scenarios; slightly less common than the equal-degree case.

in_depth.label: Reading end behavior of a rational function from its graph
in_depth.description: Uses the graph of a rational function to state the y-value that outputs approach as x → ±∞ and identifies whether a horizontal asymptote exists.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Graphical reading of asymptotic end behavior is a multiple-representation MC skill (MP2).

in_depth.label: Recognizing oblique (slant) asymptote when numerator degree is exactly one more
in_depth.description: Identifies that when deg(numerator) = deg(denominator) + 1, the end behavior is linear (oblique asymptote), and finds the oblique asymptote by polynomial division.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Oblique asymptotes appear occasionally in MC (no-calc section); less common than HA cases but included in CED 1.7 scope.

umbrellas.label: Rational Functions and Zeros
umbrellas.description: Identifies x-intercepts (zeros) of a rational function as the values where the numerator equals zero and the denominator does not; distinguishes zeros from vertical asymptotes and holes.
umbrellas.yield_rationale: CED 1.8 zeros of rationals appear in MC; interconnected with 1.9 and 1.10 — frequently tested as a system where students must distinguish zeros, VAs, and holes.

in_depth.label: Finding a zero of a rational function from a numerator factor
in_depth.description: Sets the numerator equal to zero to find candidate zeros, then verifies the denominator is nonzero at that value, confirming it is an x-intercept.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Direct zero-finding from numerator is the core procedural skill for 1.8.

in_depth.label: Distinguishing zeros from vertical asymptotes
in_depth.description: Given a rational function in factored form, classifies each root of numerator and denominator as producing either a zero (x-intercept) or a vertical asymptote, not confusing the two.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Zero/VA confusion is a persistent student error; MC distractors are built around it, making it a high-yield discrimination skill.

in_depth.label: Zero multiplicity for rational function x-intercept behavior
in_depth.description: Determines whether the graph of a rational function crosses or touches the x-axis at a zero based on the multiplicity of the numerator factor at that value.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Multiplicity at rational zeros is tested in conjunction with graph sketching; moderate MC frequency.

in_depth.label: Reading zeros of a rational function from its graph
in_depth.description: Identifies the x-intercepts of a rational function directly from its graph and confirms these correspond to numerator zeros (where the graph actually crosses the x-axis, not just approaches it).
in_depth.yield_rationale: Graphical zero identification is a standard multiple-representation MC item.

umbrellas.label: Rational Functions and Vertical Asymptotes
umbrellas.description: Identifies vertical asymptotes of a rational function as x-values where the denominator equals zero and the factor does not cancel with the numerator; describes the unbounded behavior near each asymptote.
umbrellas.yield_rationale: CED 1.9 vertical asymptotes are among the most-tested rational-function concepts; frequent MC target and occasionally appears in FRQ 1.

in_depth.label: Finding vertical asymptotes by setting denominator equal to zero
in_depth.description: Sets the denominator of a rational function equal to zero, solves for x, then checks that the numerator is nonzero there to confirm a vertical asymptote (not a hole).
in_depth.yield_rationale: Core procedural skill for 1.9; appears in nearly every MC rational-function item set.

in_depth.label: Describing unbounded behavior (→ +∞ or −∞) near a vertical asymptote
in_depth.description: Uses sign analysis to determine whether the function approaches positive or negative infinity from each side of a vertical asymptote, based on the sign of the expression near the asymptote.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Sign-of-behavior near VA is a step beyond finding the VA; tested in MC graph-reading and description items.

in_depth.label: Connecting VA behavior to multiplicity of the denominator factor
in_depth.description: Recognizes that an odd-multiplicity denominator factor causes the function to approach opposite infinities from each side (sign change), while an even-multiplicity factor causes approach to the same infinity from both sides.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Multiplicity-based VA behavior is a more nuanced MC skill; lower frequency but isolatable.

in_depth.label: Identifying vertical asymptotes from a rational function graph
in_depth.description: Reads the x-values of vertical asymptotes from a graph of a rational function by locating vertical dashed lines or unbounded behavior, and states the equations x = a.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Graphical VA identification is a standard multiple-representation MC item (MP2).

in_depth.label: Counting vertical asymptotes from a fully factored rational function
in_depth.description: Given a rational function in factored form, determines the exact number of vertical asymptotes by identifying denominator roots that do not cancel with numerator factors.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Counting VAs correctly (vs holes) requires integrating 1.9 and 1.10 reasoning; tested when factoring is required.

umbrellas.label: Rational Functions and Holes (Removable Discontinuities)
umbrellas.description: Identifies removable discontinuities (holes) in rational functions where a common factor cancels from numerator and denominator; finds the location (x-value and y-value of the hole) and distinguishes holes from vertical asymptotes.
umbrellas.yield_rationale: CED 1.10 holes appear regularly in MC; requires factoring fluency and the ability to distinguish from vertical asymptotes — a targeted discrimination skill.

in_depth.label: Identifying a hole by cancelling a common factor
in_depth.description: Factors numerator and denominator of a rational function, cancels the common factor, and identifies the x-value where the hole occurs (the zero of the cancelled factor).
in_depth.yield_rationale: Common-factor cancellation is the core 1.10 move; directly tested in MC items requiring simplification.

in_depth.label: Finding the y-coordinate of a hole
in_depth.description: Substitutes the x-value of the hole into the simplified (cancelled) form of the rational function to determine the y-coordinate of the hole.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Y-coordinate of a hole is the second step after locating it; tested in MC items asking for the ordered pair of the hole.

in_depth.label: Classifying a denominator zero as a hole vs. a vertical asymptote
in_depth.description: Given the factored form of a rational function, classifies each root of the denominator as producing either a hole (common factor cancels) or a vertical asymptote (no cancellation).
in_depth.yield_rationale: Hole/VA classification is the most common discrimination error; high MC-distractor yield.

in_depth.label: Reading holes from a graph of a rational function
in_depth.description: Identifies an open circle (removable discontinuity) on a graph of a rational function and states its coordinates as the location of the hole.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Graphical identification of holes appears in MC graph-reading items; moderate frequency.

in_depth.label: Stating the domain of a rational function, excluding holes and VAs
in_depth.description: Finds all x-values where the denominator is zero (before or after cancellation) and states the domain of the rational function as all reals excluding those values.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Domain determination for rational functions is a recurring MC skill; integrates knowledge of holes and VAs simultaneously.

umbrellas.label: Equivalent Representations of Polynomial and Rational Expressions
umbrellas.description: Rewrites polynomial and rational expressions in equivalent algebraic forms using factoring, polynomial long division, and partial fraction decomposition; connects different forms to graph features.
umbrellas.yield_rationale: CED 1.11 algebraic manipulation feeds FRQ 4 (symbolic manipulations) and MC computation items; requires procedural fluency with multiple rewriting techniques.

in_depth.label: Rewriting a polynomial expression by factoring
in_depth.description: Factors a polynomial expression (GCF, trinomial, difference of squares, sum/difference of cubes) and recognizes the factored form as an equivalent representation that exposes zeros.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Factoring as a rewriting strategy is the gateway skill for 1.11; also unlocks zeros (1.8) and holes (1.10) analysis.

in_depth.label: Using polynomial long division to rewrite a rational expression
in_depth.description: Divides a polynomial numerator by a polynomial denominator using long division to express the rational function as (quotient) + (remainder)/(divisor), revealing end behavior.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Long division is tested procedurally in MC and FRQ 4; yields the oblique/polynomial end behavior of an improper rational function.

in_depth.label: Decomposing a rational expression into partial fractions (linear denominators)
in_depth.description: Rewrites a rational expression with distinct linear factors in the denominator as a sum of simpler fractions A/(x−r1) + B/(x−r2), finding A and B by matching coefficients or substitution.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Partial fractions appear in CED 1.11 and FRQ 4 symbolic manipulation tasks; slightly lower frequency than factoring or long division. Rescored downward (from 0.62): partial fraction decomposition appears infrequently in AP Precalc FRQ 4 and is lower-priority relative to AROC and transformation skills; yield anchored in CED 1.11 rationale is lower than initially assigned.

in_depth.label: Connecting an equivalent algebraic form to a graph feature
in_depth.description: Explains how a particular equivalent form (factored, quotient, partial fraction) exposes a specific graph feature (zeros, end behavior, asymptote location).
in_depth.yield_rationale: Form-to-feature connections are MP2 items; tested in MC when students must choose the most useful form for a given task.

in_depth.label: Simplifying a rational expression by factoring and cancelling
in_depth.description: Factors both numerator and denominator of a rational expression and cancels common factors to produce an equivalent simplified form, noting any domain restrictions.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Simplification by cancellation is the most common 1.11 procedural step; bridges to hole identification (1.10) and is MC-staple.

umbrellas.label: Transformations of Functions
umbrellas.description: Constructs g(x) as a transformed version of f(x) using vertical/horizontal translations, vertical/horizontal dilations, and reflections; identifies parameter roles (a, h, k) in g(x) = af(x − h) + k.
umbrellas.yield_rationale: CED 1.12 transformations are cross-unit skills appearing in MC across all units and in every FRQ type; 0.85 from the outline plus high cross-unit FRQ applicability.

in_depth.label: Vertical translation by adding a constant outside the function
in_depth.description: Identifies that g(x) = f(x) + k shifts the graph of f upward by k units (if k > 0) or downward by |k| units (if k < 0), preserving shape and x-coordinates.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Vertical translation is the most straightforward transformation; appears in virtually every transformation MC item set.

in_depth.label: Horizontal translation by replacing x with (x − h)
in_depth.description: Identifies that g(x) = f(x − h) shifts the graph of f right by h units (if h > 0) or left by |h| units (if h < 0); recognizes the counterintuitive direction of the shift.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Horizontal shift direction (opposite sign) is the most common student error, making it high-yield for discrimination MC items.

in_depth.label: Vertical dilation and reflection by multiplying the output by a
in_depth.description: Identifies that g(x) = af(x) stretches the graph vertically by |a| (if |a| > 1), compresses it (if 0 < |a| < 1), and reflects over the x-axis if a < 0.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Vertical scaling and reflection are consistently tested together; high MC frequency across all function families.

in_depth.label: Horizontal dilation by replacing x with bx inside the function
in_depth.description: Identifies that g(x) = f(bx) compresses the graph horizontally by a factor of 1/b (if b > 1), stretches it (if 0 < b < 1), and reflects over the y-axis if b < 0.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Horizontal dilation (and its reciprocal relationship to b) is a key transformation skill tested in MC; less intuitive than vertical dilation.

in_depth.label: Applying multiple transformations in the correct order
in_depth.description: Given g(x) = af(b(x − h)) + k, identifies the sequence of transformations (horizontal shift, horizontal dilation, vertical dilation/reflection, vertical shift) and applies them in order to obtain the graph of g.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Combined transformation questions are the most common 1.12 MC form; requires sequencing multiple operations correctly.

in_depth.label: Writing the equation of a transformed function from its graph
in_depth.description: Given the graph of a transformed function and the parent function, determines the values of a, h, and k in g(x) = af(x − h) + k by identifying key feature shifts.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Inverse transformation (graph → equation) is tested in FRQs and MC; requires reading parameter values from graph features.

in_depth.label: Determining the effect of a transformation on domain and range
in_depth.description: Given a transformation g(x) = af(x − h) + k, states the new domain and range of g from the domain and range of f, accounting for shifts and reflections.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Domain/range under transformation is a focused MC sub-skill; moderate frequency in transformation item sets.

umbrellas.label: Function Model Selection and Assumption Articulation
umbrellas.description: Selects an appropriate polynomial or rational function type to model a given context; articulates assumptions, domain restrictions, and limitations of the chosen model.
umbrellas.yield_rationale: CED 1.13 is the core skill for FRQ 2 model-selection justification; also appears in MP3 communication items requiring students to state assumptions.

in_depth.label: Selecting polynomial degree from observed rate-of-change pattern
in_depth.description: Uses first/second/higher-order differences from data or context to identify whether a linear, quadratic, or higher-degree polynomial model is appropriate.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Rate-pattern model selection is the analytical step in FRQ 2 context questions; directly tests 1.3–1.4 reasoning in a modeling wrapper.

in_depth.label: Stating assumptions underlying a polynomial function model
in_depth.description: Explicitly states the mathematical assumptions (e.g., continuous behavior, no restrictions on domain, constant leading behavior) required for a chosen polynomial model to be valid in context.
in_depth.yield_rationale: FRQ 2 explicitly asks students to articulate model assumptions; this is the communication (MP3) sub-skill.

in_depth.label: Identifying contextual domain restrictions for a function model
in_depth.description: States the practical domain of a polynomial or rational model based on the context (e.g., only non-negative time values, only positive quantities), distinguishing the mathematical domain from the contextual domain.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Contextual domain restriction is tested in MC interpretation items and is a required step in FRQ 2 model-construction sub-parts.

in_depth.label: Identifying limitations of a polynomial or rational model
in_depth.description: Describes a scenario or data feature that the chosen polynomial/rational model fails to capture accurately (e.g., model predicts negative values for a quantity that must be positive, or unbounded growth).
in_depth.yield_rationale: Model-limitation sub-parts appear in FRQ 2 (justify/validate context); MP3 communication skill.

in_depth.label: Selecting a function type from the shape of a scatter plot or graph
in_depth.description: Given a scatter plot or data graph, selects whether a linear, quadratic, or rational function is the best-fit model based on visual shape features (direction of curvature, asymptotic behavior, zeros).
in_depth.yield_rationale: Visual model selection is a multiple-representation (MP2) skill tested in MC Part B (calculator allowed). Rescored downward (from 0.68): visual scatter-plot model selection is rarely isolated as a standalone MC item; usually paired with other sub-tasks.

umbrellas.label: Function Model Construction and Application
umbrellas.description: Constructs polynomial or rational function models from contextual information or data (using intercepts, a given point, or regression); applies the model to answer questions and make predictions.
umbrellas.yield_rationale: CED 1.14 is the direct target of FRQ 2 (non-periodic model construction and application); high FRQ weight and also tested in calculator-allowed MC.

in_depth.label: Constructing a polynomial model from its zeros and one additional point
in_depth.description: Writes a polynomial in factored form f(x) = a(x − r1)(x − r2)⋯ using given zeros, then uses a known point to solve for the leading coefficient a.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Zeros-plus-point construction is the canonical FRQ 2 model-building procedure; among the most directly tested 1.14 skills.

in_depth.label: Constructing a quadratic model from vertex form and an additional point
in_depth.description: Writes a quadratic model in vertex form f(x) = a(x − h)² + k using the vertex (h, k) and solves for a using a given point on the parabola.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Vertex-form construction is a clean isolated skill; frequently tested when context gives a maximum or minimum point.

in_depth.label: Applying a constructed polynomial model to answer a contextual question
in_depth.description: Evaluates the polynomial model at a given input or solves f(x) = c for x to answer a question stated in context (e.g., 'when does the height equal 50 ft?').
in_depth.yield_rationale: Model application is the final sub-part of FRQ 2; always present and directly graded.

in_depth.label: Constructing a rational function model from asymptote and point information
in_depth.description: Writes a rational function model using given asymptote locations (to infer numerator/denominator structure) and a known point to determine any remaining constants.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Rational model construction from contextual constraints appears in MC and occasionally in FRQ 2; less common than polynomial construction.

in_depth.label: Using regression technology to fit a polynomial model to data
in_depth.description: Describes the process of entering data into a graphing calculator or technology tool, selecting polynomial regression, interpreting the resulting equation, and evaluating its fit using residuals or R².
in_depth.yield_rationale: Technology-based regression is tested in calculator-allowed MC (Part B) and FRQ 2; requires procedural and interpretive fluency with regression output.

in_depth.label: Making predictions and identifying interpolation vs. extrapolation
in_depth.description: Uses a polynomial model to predict an output value within the data range (interpolation) or outside it (extrapolation), and states whether the prediction is more or less reliable based on this distinction.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Interpolation/extrapolation reliability distinction appears in FRQ 2 communication sub-parts (MP3).
